assessor_1_raw: The triage model appears to have strong detection performance for urgent findings (large pneumothorax, misplaced lines) and the queueing simulation provides a credible mechanistic argument for how worklist reordering could reduce time-to-read for urgent studies. However, the deployment memo claims the model will 'improve patient outcomes' — a much stronger claim than 'reduce time-to-read.' The gap between these claims is the pathway from earlier radiologist review to actual patient outcome: clinician notification, treatment initiation, and the time-sensitivity of the specific findings. This pathway is not analyzed. The evidence supports the first link in the causal chain; the subsequent links are assumed.
assessor_2_raw: The queueing simulation has a major scope limitation: it models only the worklist step. Real clinical impact depends on the complete pathway from imaging to treatment. For a large pneumothorax requiring urgent drainage, the time from study to treatment involves: (1) radiologist reads and reports, (2) clinician is notified, (3) patient is transferred or prepared, (4) procedure is performed. If steps 2-4 take an hour regardless of when step 1 occurs, earlier reading does not accelerate treatment. Hospitals also typically have parallel escalation protocols for the highest-acuity findings — direct calls from technologists to on-call radiologists. The simulation does not account for cases already handled outside the standard worklist. The model's incremental benefit may be primarily for less-critical urgent findings where direct escalation is not standard practice.
assessor_3_raw: The correct framing for this tool is: 'simulation evidence supports that urgent studies would move earlier in the radiology worklist; whether this translates to outcome improvement requires prospective deployment measurement.' The memo's framing as a 'patient-outcome-improving intervention' is premature. The simulation also has an internal assumption issue: it assumes radiologists interpret in model-priority order, which may not hold in practice. A prospective deployment with randomized unit allocation and outcome measurement would provide the evidence needed to validate or refute the outcome claim.
synthesis_raw:
SYNTHESIS — scope_intent_radiology_queue_302 — Ensemble Run 1

All three assessors identify the same pattern: the model has plausible mechanism (worklist reordering) with undemonstrated outcome efficacy. This is a mixed verdict — the critique issues are real, but the defender position (mechanism is plausible and worth testing) is also credible.

The issues are empirically resolvable:
- Issue 1 (outcome_claim_exceeds_evidence): resolvable by prospective deployment with outcome measurement
- Issue 2 (unmodeled_downstream_workflow): resolvable by end-to-end time analysis in deployment
- Issue 3 (simulation_not_deployment): inherently requires deployment to address

Empirical test specification:
- Measure: prospective deployment in one radiology unit (90 days, randomized urgent/non-urgent image allocation); measure time-to-first-read for model-flagged urgent studies vs. control; time from image acquisition to treatment initiation for same cases; proportion of urgent cases already escalated via parallel pathways pre-deployment
- Success criterion: statistically significant reduction in time-to-first-read AND time-to-treatment for urgent finding categories; parallel pathway escalation accounts for <50% of urgency-driven cases
- Failure criterion: time-to-first-read improves but time-to-treatment unchanged (downstream bottleneck); OR >50% of model-flagged urgent cases already escalated via parallel protocols pre-deployment (redundant with existing workflow)

Verdict: EMPIRICAL_TEST_AGREED

empirical_test.measure: 90-day prospective deployment: time-to-first-read for urgent studies; time acquisition-to-treatment; proportion already escalated via parallel pathways
empirical_test.success_criterion: significant reduction in time-to-read AND time-to-treatment; parallel escalation accounts for <50% of cases
empirical_test.failure_criterion: time-to-read improves but time-to-treatment unchanged; or >50% of model-flagged urgent cases already managed via direct escalation
